text: Welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. Today with me I have Grant
from ACV Actions. Hi Grant, how are you doing? Hi, I'm well, thanks. So Grant, tell me a little
bit of what ACV does. Sure, so ACV is an automotive platform and marketplace and we
primarily facilitate online auctions and vehicle inspections for wholesale dealerships across the
US. Very nice. So actions and deals, that sounds like a lot. Can you show me, can we dive into the
architecture on how you handle that? Sure, so it's definitely a lot. We're currently serving hundreds
of millions of requests per month. And the way we actually end up serving that is through our
mobile clients and our web-facing clients. So what happens is every request comes through load balancers
and those end up getting spread down into all of our different microservices that are hosted on our
EKS clusters. I see. What kind of microservices, what kind of services do you have there? Sure, so our
microservices can be anything from I need to, you know, facilitate the timers in an auction, to I
need to launch the actual auction, to I'm a user, I need to place a bid, to I need to send push
notifications, to give me transportation quotes for transporting a vehicle across the states. That
sounds like a lot. Are all of those services, are they individual scaling or? Yeah, so they're all
individual. So we have about a dozen or so domain teams that run these 50 or so different microservices
across all of our different, you know, production clusters or whatnot.
And all of those individuals, what is that themselves? How did you design them? Are they all the same? Do they
use the same compute or? So no, yeah, no two microservices are alike. So what we have in our
in our Kubernetes clusters are multiple node groups. So whether it's, you know, a microservice that's
really, you know, CPU intensive, they might be scheduled on a compute node group. And we even had
node groups or things like, you know, machine learning to do GPU based, you know, instances in our
Kubernetes clusters and whatnot. Oh, interesting. And is there a specific reason why you use EKS to
actually design this infrastructure? Sure. Scale. Scale is the number one thing. So like I said earlier,
we're serving hundreds of millions of requests per month right now. And we plan on serving a lot
more in the future. So scale, you know, we have dozens of teams that are producing microservices and
shipping to production hundreds of times a week. And we really needed a containerized architecture to
kind of meet that, meet that expectation. Wow, that's a lot, a lot of teams working together.
It also sounds like a lot of data come in here. Do you preserve that data at some point? Do you have a
single source as true? Yep, absolutely. So each one of our domain teams is given RDS instances across our
different environments and whatnot. And these RDS instances can communicate with our EKS clusters and
whatnot. So they're given the freedom to kind of architect how they want their databases to line
up within that instance, within that scope. And then they can use that to persist whatever data their
services actually need. Right. And you mentioned the services and the outwork together. How do they
communicate? Sure. So they communicate primarily through our Kafka. So in Kubernetes, we provide
sidecar containers that essentially provide communication to our Kafka clusters to a service,
you know, we call Data Hub. And essentially what that is, is, is a way for all of our services to
produce and consume events back and forth from Kafka clusters. So we, you know, one service produces an
event to say, hey, maybe you placed a bid. And another service consumes that event and says, I need to
maybe write that bid to the database or something. Nice. And you're using Kafka then as a message bus
for all of it, but to communicate between all of your microservices. Then are you using Kafka for
anything else? Absolutely. Yeah. So our second, our second main use case for Data Hub is our, is our
analytics team essentially. So like I said before, we're producing all of these events from all of our
different microservices, like for something like bidding, for example, but our analytics team really needs
to know this information to make business decisions in as near real time as they possibly can get.
So what we've been able to produce here is, is a system where a microservice can produce an event
to Kafka and our analytics team can either, you know, literally watch that live stream off Kafka and
watch live events, or they can build their own consumers down here and build out projections
that they can then build, you know, reporting dashboards and whatnot to, to make business decisions.
I see. So you're using Kafka as a message bus and also as a reporting. What kind of analytics is your
team running on them besides the live stream? Sure. A great example is, is sell through. So we,
we launched thousands of auctions a day and not all of them sell and some of them need to run multiple
times to sell and whatnot. And it's a really important business metric to understand what our
current sell through is at, you know, what, what actually dealers are selling cars, what dealers are
not selling cars, who's buying those kinds of things. So right now, Grant, you're using Kafka for
messaging, monitoring, and analytics. It's very nice. I love your architecture. Thank you for sharing this with us.
Thank you. And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
Thank you.
Thanks, Scott.
Thanks.
